# Budget is in the dark as Hormuz oil crisis upends economic outlook

By [Alan Kohler](https://www.abc.net.au/news/alan-kohler/167112)

- [Topic:Budget](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/budget)

17h ago17 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 2:30pm

![A man in a suit from a side profile with his chin jutted out defiantly.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c1c7302a6849b1174a8dbbe44b7bfe01?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=900&cropW=1600&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

If the Strait of Hormuz is still closed by June, policymakers like Treasurer Jim Chalmers will face tough decisions. (ABC News: Luke Stephenson)

It looks like the 2026 federal budget will have to be prepared over the next fortnight with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed and the global economy in flux.

At least Treasury will be able to assume that the biggest single budget problem, the NDIS, has been fixed.

That's the benefit of pre-budget announcements like last week's proposed NDIS reforms: Treasury modellers can do the forward estimates on the basis that 160,000 people will no longer be on the NDIS, and the cost will be $15 billion a year less than it was when they last ran the numbers five months ago.

Whether Health Minister Mark Butler and NDIS Minister Jenny McAllister can actually pull that off is neither here nor there — it's announced, therefore it is, to [paraphrase Rene Descartes](https://humanidades.com/en/i-think-therefore-i-am/).

That can't be said of Donald Trump's announcement on Truth Social over the weekend that the United States has " [total control](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/23/trump-claims-us-has-total-control-over-strait-of-hormuz-as-iran-seizes-two-container-ships) over the Strait of Hormuz".

Maybe they do, maybe they don't, but so does Iran.

We seem to have entered a sort of hostage/siege phase of this war, where Iran has taken the Strait of Hormuz hostage, and the United States has besieged Iran, or at least its ports. "It is " [Sealed up Tight](https://www.bbc.com/audio/play/w1730c69yn9x0b7)," posted Trump, "until such time as Iran is able to make a DEAL!!!"

The problem is that neither combatant is sufficiently hurting the other for a deal to be urgent enough for the rest of us.

## The big question

Iran's closure of the strait is not hurting the US directly at all. As Trump himself said: "The United States [imports almost no oil](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48903) through the Hormuz Strait and won't be taking any in the future. We don't need it. We haven't needed it, and we don't need it."

Sieges only work, or don't take a long time, if the enemy can be starved. America's siege of Iran's ports is painful, but Iran won't starve.

Trump says Iran is losing $US500 million a day ($700 million) as a result of the blockade, but that is a big exaggeration.

Before the war, Iran was selling 1.6 million barrels of oil a day for about $US65 a barrel, or a total of about $US100 million. Even at the current price, it would be only about $US150 million, but that's hypothetical, and not what they're "losing", and while the lack of imports is also hurting, the total cost is nowhere near $US500 million per day.

Not that Iran can last forever, but an economic analyst and professor at Shahid Beheshti University in Tehran, Saeed Laylaz, [told Agence France-Presse](https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20260423-iran-economy-looks-set-to-withstand-us-naval-blockade) last week: "If the blockade lasts for more than two or three months, it can cause more damage." He didn't say Iran can't survive more than two or three months, but only that it will cause "more damage".

On the other hand, two or three months without any exports from the Persian Gulf would cause a great deal of damage to the global economy, probably a recession.

It's not just oil and therefore the diesel and jet fuel needed for logistics and air travel, which is bad enough, but the price of plastic packaging is now increasing rapidly because naphtha is in short supply, and food prices are rising because of a collapse in the supply of urea fertilisers.

So the question for the treasurer, Jim Chalmers, and the Treasury secretary, Jenny Wilkinson, is: If the Strait of Hormuz is still closed on May 12, when does the budget assume it will reopen?

## A budget that doesn't have the numbers

Trump said he would send his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and a real estate developer named Steve Witkoff, back to the Serena Hotel in Islamabad to resume negotiations, but when the Iranians refused to negotiate while under blockade, he cancelled their trip. So with the clock ticking on the budget, there are no talks going on at all.

Maybe they will turn up and negotiate, and maybe one side, or both, will fold within two weeks, but that seems very unlikely.

Much more likely is that on budget night, Treasury boffins will still have no idea what numbers to plug into their budget model for the oil price, gas exports, plastic and food prices, inflation, nominal GDP, and tax revenue.

In other words, this year's budget will be a lot worse than "uncertain", as Chalmers has been warning — it will basically be irrelevant, a sort of holding document pending another budget once the status of exports out of the Persian Gulf is settled.

With the hardline Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) now in charge in Iran, and with power apparently split among 31 regional commanders, getting some kind of quick capitulation from Iran seems out of the question.

The most likely scenario is that Trump declares victory at some point and goes home, probably with the same deal that was signed in 2015, the one known as the JCPOA, Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action.

## And a ceasefire without peace or stability

Iran would end up like the Korean Peninsula after the Korean War — that is, a ceasefire without peace. Like North Korea, Iran would go flat out to develop a nuclear bomb.

That would probably encourage others in the region to do the same, like Türkiye and Saudi Arabia.

The Middle East would become an even more dangerous and volatile mess, with other Persian Gulf countries forced to pay Iran's toll to export and import through the Strait of Hormuz. Global shipping would be destabilised, and the oil price would stay high.

The only scenario that would not mean higher inflation and lower economic growth — stagflation — is one in which the IRGC quickly loses its grip on power and Iran's military dictatorship ends. Right now, that scenario looks the least likely.

## The future is complex

Accompanying this oil shock (and gas, plastics and fertiliser shock) is a profound change in the way fiscal and monetary policymakers operate.

Recessions used to be seen as "cleansing" events, necessary to reset the economy and control inflation.

In 1979, Fed chairman Paul Volcker did not hesitate to crush the US and world economies to remove the inflation that had become embedded after two oil shocks in the 1970s. And Paul Keating will always be remembered for telling us in November 1990 that the recession, then underway, was the one we had to have.

The global financial crisis changed all that. The 2008 "great recession" (except in Australia) was caused by a collapse in demand, in turn caused by a financial crisis, not the sort of supply shortage of the 70s or the terms of trade crisis of the late-80s, so policymakers agreed that demand had to be supported in 2008-09, not retrenched.

Budget deficits exploded and interest rates were slashed, but after the crisis, government debt was not reduced and interest rates were not put back where they were, and in fact, when inflation stayed low after 2013, rates were cut below what they had been in the financial crisis.

The COVID-19 pandemic saw a collapse in both supply and demand, but policymakers focused on boosting demand, cutting interest rates to zero and below zero, and pumping up fiscal support again.

That rejection of economic rationalism has since been reinforced by the rise of political populism, both left and right, as technocratic, administrative political classes try to hold power with fiscal generosity, while those countries that have succumbed to populism — like the United States — do the same, except worse.

It's probably going too far to say that recessions have been abolished by political and economic elites, but central banks and governments are much keener to avoid them now than they were 30 years ago.

So if the Strait of Hormuz is still closed as April turns into May and then, God forbid, June and beyond, policymakers will have a tough decision.

They will face the clearest supply-side stagflation since the 1970s, and boosting demand with rate cuts and fiscal stimulus as they did during the global financial crisis and pandemic would only make the inflation, and the eventual reckoning, worse.

Jim Chalmers might have to preside over another recession we have to have.

**Alan Kohler is a finance presenter and columnist for ABC News and he also writes for Intelligent Investor.**

# Australia's minimum fuel reserves would double under $800m Coalition plan, at a cost to drivers

By chief digital political correspondent [Clare Armstrong](https://www.abc.net.au/news/clare-armstrong/105969816)

- [Topic:Federal Government](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/federal-government)

12h ago12 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 7:42am

![Angus Taylor speaking to media standing outside a building.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/728be7096f879ca6e54f2a6a08a26f54?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2250&cropW=4000&xPos=0&yPos=87&width=862&height=485)

Angus Taylor's latest policy offering is focused around increasing Australia's fuel reserves. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

## In short:

The opposition has called on the federal government to double minimum fuel reserves held in Australia and boost storage capacity in response to the global oil crisis.

The Coalition's fuel policy would mean Australia always has at least 60 days of diesel, petrol and jet fuel on shore, but the plan would add to the cost of fuel.

## What's next?

Labor says energy resilience will be an important part of the federal budget due to be released on May 12.

Drivers would have to pay an extra one cent per litre at the petrol pump under a new Coalition plan to boost Australia's onshore fuel reserves.

The Coalition's $800 million energy security policy, to be unveiled by Opposition Leader Angus Taylor today, sets out a plan to increase Australia's fuel stocks to a 60-day supply, with at least 1 billion litres of additional storage capacity.

But Nationals leader Matt Canavan has revealed the $800 million outlined in the policy would not cover the cost of increasing the storage capacity to what would be required, which he said was an estimated 4.4 billion litres.

"The total cost would be around $6 billion, along with both the storage, extra storage costs, and the cost of actually filling the storage and maintaining that," he told AM.

"It wouldn't fund the entire amount of that extra storage, but it would help make a contribution, help ease the transition here over a few years.

"We're suggesting that $800 million from Export Finance Australia, which is a government bank effectively, be used to help the private sector fund this."

He said that funding would happen in the form of loans.

After the war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz exposed Australia's vulnerability to global oil supply shocks, Mr Canavan said the one-cent-per-litre cost was a price people would be willing to bear.

"That is an extra cost, we recognise that, but given what we're facing right now, we think it's a reasonable insurance premium to improve the security of all Australians."

The move would bring Australia closer to the 90 days of storage agreed to under International Energy Agency agreements.

Resources Minister Madeleine King has previously said reaching that threshold would come at an estimated cost of $20 billion.

## Double the stockpile

As Labor prepares to hand down its own response at the federal budget on May 12, Mr Taylor will announce that a future Coalition government will aim to double the minimum stockholding obligation to 60 days by 2030.

This would require the nation's fuel importers and refineries to develop significantly more storage capacity, which Mr Taylor said the Coalition would support through the new $800 million Australian Fuel Security Facility.

Under the plan, the funds would deliver at least an extra 1 billion litres in onshore storage for petrol, diesel and jet fuel.

![Large tanks at an oil refinery.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/fb50520e01c46c2b5818ced90e0dc4c1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3808&cropW=5712&xPos=0&yPos=238&width=862&height=575)

Three diesel oil storage tanks holding 30 million litres of fuel. (ABC News: Andy Ware)

Mr Taylor is also calling on Labor to lift the baseline fuel stockholding levels from January 1, 2027 to lock in the current average level as the new minimum.

Energy and Climate Change Minister Chris Bowen revealed on Sunday that Australia had 44 days' worth of petrol, 33 days of diesel, and 30 days of jet fuel in reserve.

Mr Taylor said the Coalition was putting forward a "practical plan" to make sure Australia could "stand on its own two feet" regarding fuel supply.

"This is a plan the prime minister should pick up today. No excuses, no delays," Mr Taylor said.

> "If fuel stops, Australia stops. It's that simple. Trucks don't move, supermarkets don't stock, businesses shut their doors."

Mr Canavan said families and businesses needed confidence that fuel would be there when they needed it.

"People in the regions know how serious this is. If the diesel doesn't turn up, the farm doesn't run, and the shelves go empty," he said.

"This plan is just common sense. Keep more fuel here in Australia so we are not relying on overseas supply lines that can be cut overnight.

"We cannot keep hoping for the best. We need to be ready, and this plan gets us there."

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 13 minutes 16 seconds.

Watch 13m

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e1bc0b15f5a438a6fce77fefde42aed0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2160&cropW=3840&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Australia negotiates with overseas fuel suppliers as the fuel crisis continues. (ABC 7:30: Jacob Greber and Sarah Ferguson)

Shadow Minister for Energy and Emissions Reduction Dan Tehan said the plan would work alongside industry, build storage where it was needed, and make sure Australia had a "buffer to withstand global shocks".

Members of the International Energy Agency, including Australia, are technically required to hold oil stocks equal to at least 90 days of net imports to provide a buffer that can be released during global supply disruptions.

The Coalition has claimed its policy to lift the minimum stockholding obligation would put Australia within "range" of achieving that 90-day ambition.

## Australia's fuel stockpile higher than when Iran war began

Mr Canavan suggests the Coalition would back the construction of new refineries, particularly projects prioritising diesel refining.

"We should support a new refinery," he said.

The Coalition would, Mr Canavan said, offer government support to a new refinery by extending current subsidies already offered to the country's existing two.

He said the Coalition would "provide the environment, the fertile ground to encourage someone to build a new refinery", but didn't go as far as announcing specific financial support above what was already available.

Mr Canavan pointed to a proposal for a Gladstone refinery as a project worth supporting, as well as the potential for another "out in the west" where there's high demand for diesel.

![Matt Canavan](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6e51f142e969d9f90d04f2c3ba37b8a9?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3512&cropW=5268&xPos=486&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Matt Canavan says the Coalition would provide fertile ground for the development of new refineries. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

Over the weekend, Mr Bowen revealed Australia had more petrol in reserve than before the Iran war began, confirming supply continued to arrive, with 58 oil tankers on their way to Australia.

"As this international crisis continues with… uncertainty around the world about when and how it will end, the government will continue to take every step necessary to ensure security of fuel supply to Australia," he said.

Labor has indicated Australia's energy resilience will be a feature of the upcoming federal budget, but Mr Bowen has not foreshadowed specific measures.

"I don't comment on budget deliberations other than to say this government's commitment to resilience and sovereignty through the 'Future Made in Australia' agenda is very, very strong and very, very clear," he said earlier this month.

Mr Bowen said the government had already announced commitments to support Australia's two remaining oil refineries in Brisbane and Geelong, and has played down the prospect of Labor funding another facility.

He acknowledged the country would have been in a better position to deal with the oil crisis sparked by the Iran war if other refineries had not been shuttered in the past decade, which he blamed on the past Coalition government.

Mr Bowen last week indicated it was not realistic for Australia to build another oil refinery, but he has left the door open to expanding the capacity of two existing facilities.

"It's a lot cheaper to intervene, to save the refinery, while it's still there, than to rebuild one after it's been dismantled," Mr Bowen told The Australian.

"So, the idea that we would come along and rebuild a refinery that's been closed for 10 years is not one that I would urge people to regard as, ultimately, very realistic."

## Energy security tops Japan, China, South Korea agenda for Wong

Foreign Minister Penny Wong on Monday departed for Japan, where she plans to discuss energy security and how to coordinate during the oil crisis, before travelling to China and South Korea by the end of the week.

![Two men and a woman in suits stand at a podium speaking to journalists with the Australian flag in the background.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c946468fdb6623737f47dde16cfd0743?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=900&cropW=1350&xPos=100&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Penny Wong is travelling to Japan, China and South Korea for talks this week. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

"We want to remain a reliable \[energy and food\] supplier, and we want to see Australia prioritised when it comes to diesel, petrol and fertiliser," Senator Wong said.

"That requires face-to-face engagement."

Senator Wong said the Asia region, which gets about 80 per cent of its oil through the Strait of Hormuz, was "disproportionately affected" by the critical shipping lane's ongoing closure.

"We want to see the crisis de-escalate, we want to see negotiations, and we want to see the strait open for the benefit of Australians, \[and\] for the benefit of the people of the world," she said.

# New inflation data drops tomorrow. Be ready with Sensible Emily's hip pocket guide to the CPI

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/307a5d1c7aff8a518b4770599bd5334f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3171&cropW=3171&xPos=0&yPos=213&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
E\\
\\
By Emily Stewart](https://www.abc.net.au/news/emily-stewart/4506536)
- [Topic:Personal Finance](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/personal-finance)

5h ago5 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 2:58pm

![GRAPHIC Emily Stewart falling notes](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ffc62844032c37154945aaa1b1f7b03d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=911&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=37&width=862&height=485)

_Hi, I'm_ [_Emily Stewart_](https://www.abc.net.au/news/emily-stewart/4506536) _and I'm here to help you over the next couple of weeks as we navigate some of the **important moments** in economic news that could **affect you**._ 🫶

_The war in Iran has caused so much **uncertainty** globally and I know it can be an **anxious** time as we hear about how the oil crisis has affected us here in Australia._ 😟

_Some of those effects are immediately obvious, like the higher price of petrol. But some things take time to become clear — like the impact on **inflation** figures, **interest rates** and the **federal budget.**_ 💰

_I'm here to help you understand what's happening. If you have a question for me,_ [_**please reach out**_](https://yourquestions.abc.net.au/hc/en-au/requests/new?ticket_form_id=13196881880719) _. I'd love to answer your questions_. 🤔

## What is the key moment I should look out for on inflation?

- **What:** The Australian Bureau of Statistics will release the latest **Consumer Price Index** (CPI) data
- **When:** Tomorrow (April 29)
- **Why is this important?** The CPI tracks how much we pay for a hypothetical "basket" of goods and services we have to buy regularly. 🧺 It matters because it's what the Reserve Bank looks at when it's deciding whether to increase or decrease interest rates.

![GRAPHIC Basket of goods on green](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c47180aac18c33b64e16e649833d6bac?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

## What is the Consumer Price Index?

- **What is it?** The CPI is the official measure of household inflation and tracks changes in the **cost of living** for consumers. 💵 It looks at a **broad range of products** and services, including housing, food and education. The Australian Bureau of Statistics collects the data monthly using supermarket scanning data, web scraping and even phone collection from retailers. As of October last year, it **publishes the data monthly**, to give everyone a "quicker read" on inflation. However, monthly data is more volatile than quarterly figures (so take it with a grain of salt🧂).
- **Why does it matter?** It not only confirms our fears about how much products and services have increased in price, but it can also ultimately have a big **impact** on how much we end up paying in **interest on our mortgage** 🏠 (or how much interest we earn on savings).
- **How could it affect me?** If prices climb faster than your income, you can't afford to buy as much. This isn't good news for business either. So, the Reserve Bank tries to keep inflation in the 2-3 per cent range. If it starts getting too high, the bank might decide to increase interest rates to try and encourage Australians to spend less.

"All groups CPI" refers to headline inflation. The "trimmed mean" is the RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation. Annual movements prior to April 2025 are calculated by comparing each quarter to the same quarter in the previous year. From April 2025 these movements are calculated by comparing each month to the same month in the previous year.

Source:Chart: ABC News Source: Australian Bureau of StatisticsGet the data

## Should I be worried?

Economists (and financial markets) will be taking **particular interest** in tomorrow's CPI data because it's the first set of data that will **capture the impact of the oil price spike** caused by the war in Iran.⛽

The **CPI rose 3.7 per cent in February** (down slightly from 3.8 per cent in January). Underlying inflation, (RBA's preferred measure because it strips out large price movements) was steady at 3.3 per cent. However, that is **already higher than the Reserve Bank's target range**(see the graph above). Now, the increased **fuel prices** are likely to **push March inflation higher** again. (We've actually just seen this happen in the UK💂).

So, most economists reckon the Reserve Bank is **likely to raise interest rates** again in May, in order to get inflation back down. However, don't forget **inflation is only one key set of data**. The RBA will also be looking at **unemployment** figures and **economic growth** to decide on the official cash rate.💸

Some economists are already warning that we could be starting to see something called " [**stagflation**](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-22/what-is-stagflation-and-what-can-we-do-about-it/106472996)" (a stagnant economy with high inflation). However, others believe the oil price spike might only cause a short-lived inflation peak.📈

So, I think at this stage, **be alert, but not alarmed**. If inflation increases, interest rates are likely to as well. Try and adjust your budget and shop around for the best deal.🛒

## What is the next key moment to watch for?

- **What:** The Reserve Bank will meet next week and announce whether or not **interest rates** will rise.
- **When:** Tuesday May 5 at 2.30pm AEST
- **Why is it important?** The official cash rate decision flows through to how much your bank charges you in interest on your mortgage, or pays you in interest on your savings.

_I'll be back soon to help you understand the CPI announcement. Until then, you can_ [_**follow me for more**_](https://www.abc.net.au/newsletters?isfeatured=abc-your-money-explained) _personal finance news_.

Disclaimer: This information is just general in nature. If you need personalised financial advice, please see a professional.

# Trump is political kryptonite in Australia but diplomacy still guides the way

By [Patricia Karvelas](https://www.abc.net.au/news/patricia-karvelas/6086082)

- [Topic:Foreign Affairs](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/foreign-affairs)

21h ago21 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 2:30pm

![Anthony Albanese takes a selfie with a smiling Donald Trump](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2cd5232e088ec6df609efda27359fb75?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=344&cropW=611&xPos=23&yPos=66&width=862&height=485)

Anthony Albanese must walk a careful line: keeping voters convinced of the merits of the US alliance, while not giving the impression he is being too reverential to Donald Trump. (Instagram: @Albomp)

The annual dinner for White House journalists has become the latest scene in the cycle of political violence we have come to expect from the country that is our greatest ally, with armed security rushing the event.

Attendees were mid-meal when a member of the White House security detail appeared on the ballroom floor and yelled for everyone to get down, with Secret Service agents shouting "shots fired", "stay down", and "out of the way, sir!" as US President Donald Trump and cabinet members were rushed out.

The cancer of political extremism and violence continues to infect the US, and Australians look across the Pacific with worry about the state of the world and the democratic health of a country we need and rely on to remain a healthy democracy.

## Political kryptonite in Australia

The news of Trump surviving [yet another brush](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2026/04/25/trump-threats-assassination-attempts/89804208007/) with an armed shooter comes as new figures reveal just how contentious he is here.

A staggering 66 per cent of Australians disapprove of the way Trump is doing his job, up from 51 per cent disapproval late last year, with the controversial leader political kryptonite in Australia.

Polling by Talbot Mills Research, one of the research firms used by Labor, found the US president has dramatically lost support in Australia. Approval of Trump has fallen to 27 per cent, from 40 per cent in October last year.

The survey of more than 1,000 Australians taken this month, during the midst of the US/Israeli conflict with Iran, shows an increasing hostility to Trump.

The risk that his unpopularity starts infecting the Australian government should be a real concern for Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, who has been locked into ensuring our alliance with the US is maintained.

The government believes it has never been more important for Australia's long-term interests to stay close to the US, but also recognises that being besties with Trump risks covering themselves in electoral repellent.

Albanese needs to walk a careful line in how he keeps voters convinced of the merits of the alliance, while not giving the impression he is being too reverential to Trump.

![Donald Trump points his finger at a colleague, he is sitting and dressed in a navy suit.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/078e146bb1a66e56a91227afdedef097?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

In Australia, approval of Trump has fallen to 27 per cent, from 40 per cent in October last year. (Reuters: Kylie Cooper)

## Wide-spread anxiety in the community

When asked about the world becoming a much more unstable and dangerous place, 65 per cent of those surveyed said they were concerned about the issue — up from 59 per cent in January.

Those figures reflect the genuine and widespread community anxiety that has been bubbling away since the pandemic, with a sense that life has never really returned to normal and new and complex problems keep turning up at our door.

The war in Iran has not only resulted in fresh violence in the Middle East, but what started with worries about our fuel reserves is now quickly turning into broader angst about our supply chain.

Albanese has been effective in working with our regional neighbours to secure deals for more fuel, fertiliser and other essential products, but it is not lost on many that the reason we are in this tight squeeze is because the Iran War has shut down the critical shipping passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

The Australian government deployed a Wedgetail aircraft, defence personnel and missiles to support the US in the conflict, back in March, and was deliberate and repeated in framing its role as defensive and about protecting Australians in the region living in countries like the UAE.

The government has been firm on that nuance, aware that the distinction between offering support and engaging in offensive operations that look like we are following the US into another war risks not just community concern, but also a backlash from those in Labor's left.

## Leading with diplomacy

Against this backdrop, Albanese has reaffirmed his willingness to host Trump in Australia. The PM has done so despite the fractures emerging in the relationship as a result of the Iran conflict, including Trump being critical of Australia for not doing enough.

When asked if he had spoken to Trump, the prime minister confirmed he had spoken to him since the conflict started and described the exchange as constructive.

He also confirmed Australia remains in regular contact with the Trump administration.

"I had the conversation during this conflict with President Trump. It was a good conversation, it was a constructive conversation, as all of mine have been with him," Albanese told the ABC.

That diplomacy comes despite Trump publicly expressing dissatisfaction with Australia's contribution in the Middle East.

Albanese, for his part, is continuing to play a straight bat, with the standing invitation for Trump to visit Australia unchanged.

"Of course it is open … he knows where Australia is," the prime minister said.

"An American president would always be welcome in Australia, including President Trump. He would [always be welcome here](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-23/albanese-on-us-ties-amid-war-in-iran/106598696), as would any American president."

The prime minister's remarks signal an effort to maintain diplomatic stability as the conflict in the region drags on and gets more complex.

Albanese also backed moves to extend the ceasefire between the United States and Iran, reiterating calls for an end to the conflict.

"I just hope \[the end\] is as soon as possible," he said.

"We have called for de-escalation … I am absolutely happy with \[the ceasefire extension and\] anything that is about de-escalation, because quite clearly what I want to see is an end to the human impact, of course that it's having as well, but the economic impact."

Loading

## Domestic political fallout

That Albanese talks about the economic impact in the context of the conflict is no mistake. It has already been seized on and weaponised at home.

Advocacy group GetUp has raised more than $400,000 to target Pauline Hanson for her affinity to Trump in the battleground electorate of Farrer.

Binding Hanson with Trump, the campaign seeks to lay blame on him and by default her for the rising costs hitting regional voters, particularly fuel and fertiliser.

The populist party has staked its ambitions on candidate David Farley to win its first federal lower house contest — and become the party's second MP after a rapid surge in support over the summer.

GetUp's ads — plastered across billboards, television, radio and digital media — aim to dash these hopes by highlighting Hanson's links to Mr Trump and mining billionaire Gina Rinehart.

The fact that the Iran conflict has spilled into an issue playing out in the key Farrer by-election, to be held on May 9, highlights the stakes for Albanese.

It isn't just managing the alliance with the US, but also the force currently occupying the Oval Office.

**Patricia Karvelas is host of ABC News Afternoon Briefing at 4pm weekdays on ABC News Channel, co-host of the weekly Party Room podcast with Fran Kelly and host of politics and news podcast Politics Now.**

# Apple's Tim Cook another casualty as worries about AI's impact on jobs grow

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/9442f22b8be2e1288bc1e159b86e1769?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1149&cropW=1148&xPos=900&yPos=205&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
I\\
\\
By Ian Verrender](https://www.abc.net.au/news/ian-verrender/5261264)
- [Topic:AI](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/ai)

4h ago4 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 2:47pm

![Tim Cook stands in front of a screen with an Apple logo.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cb9a859dbb308e429bccc8a199cb9150?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1687&cropW=2998&xPos=2&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Tim Cook is a cautious and thoughtful man who has prioritised user privacy. (AP: Apple)

His departure, seemingly without warning, stunned the world. But Tim Cook's time had come, cut short by his perceived unwillingness to confront a technological shift that now has swallowed him.

For a man who helped usher in Apple's communications revolution, it was the ultimate irony.

More than 1.5 billion people across the planet feel completely dependent on the technology he pioneered, and, if not physically attached, consider their iPhone an essential part of everyday life.

Add in another billion or so Apple products out there, from Macs to iPads, AirPods, and even the odd remaining iPod, and it's little wonder the devotion many consumers had to the products was embraced in equal measure by shareholders.

His replacement by long-time [Apple executive John Ternus marks a turning point for the company](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-21/who-is-john-ternus-the-new-ceo-apple/106586802) with profound implications for global investors, including our very own superannuation giants.

![John Ternus gestures with his hands, wears dark tee, short dark hair, Caucasian, large screen behind with colours.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/649364cd02081df690f6cd463b28ca4d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2787&cropW=4181&xPos=0&yPos=277&width=862&height=575)

John Ternus will become Apple's next chief executive officer on September 1. (Reuters: File)

Apple has been a key contributor to the incredible growth in Australian retirement savings, particularly since the global financial crisis.

The numbers tell the story.

[Under Cook's 14-year reign](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-21/tim-cook-steps-down-as-apple-ceo/106586654), Apple's market valuation soared from $US350 billion ($488 billion) to more than $US4 trillion ($5.6 trillion). Revenue quadrupled to $US416 billion ($580 billion).

But at some point, in the past few years, Cook was left behind.

A cautious and thoughtful man who prioritised user privacy, a once winning combination that helped catapult Apple into global corporate dominance, Cook's approach was perceived as a dragging anchor in the race for artificial intelligence (AI) domination

Apple has always had an insular approach. It forged its own pathway, developing technology and even its own chips in-house. Its hardware outlasted the opposition.

But around this time last year, investors began asking questions.  Why was Cook underspending on AI development, especially considering the frenzied activity of his competitors?

And why was he content to let Siri, the AI application first introduced by Apple back in 2010, languish?

## Apple scruffs

Cook may well have made the right choice.

At the beginning of this year, Apple's main competitors — Amazon, Alphabet, Meta and Microsoft — pledged to outlay a combined $US650 billion in capital expenditure, most of which will be directed to AI.

The thinking within Apple, or at least by Cook, was that AI would become a mere commodity to be bought and sold.

Cook hooked Apple up with long-time nemesis [Google to provide AI technology for Siri](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-06-11/apple-ai-chatgpt-siri-new-york-ban-addictive-social-media-kids/103962886) for what is believed to be about $US1 billion ($1.4 billion), a mere fraction of Google's AI outlay and a pittance when compared to the industry splurge.

![A man walks past a multi-coloured logo spelling Google.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2cc37d43728e82b234d7629ab147c338?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Google has invested heavily in AI technology. (Getty: Alain Jocard)

But for many, that financially conservative approach threatened to undermine the very essence of Apple's existence; its independence.

Google technology powers Android phones. There were fears that allowing the rival to power a key segment of its own platform, the intelligence side, may sideline Apple into merely providing hardware.

As concerns mounted midway through last year, Apple shares were slammed before a Christmas recovery. This year, they've flatlined.

On an annual basis, Apple delivered a 29 per cent return, which doesn't sound unreasonable. But it pales in comparison with Google parent Alphabet, which delivered 114 per cent growth.

## Is your money putting you out of work?

There's a lot riding on Cook's replacement, John Ternus, a man who has been involved in every major product release since he joined in 2001.

Australian investors have about $9 billion invested in the company through our major superannuation funds. Australian Super accounts for about one-third of that.

Like many global investors, [a large chunk of Australia's $4 trillion super pot](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-02-10/ai-disrupts-tech-boom-shares-drop/106323844) has been directed towards AI and the handful of US corporations that dominate the sphere.

The most recent estimates are that Australian exposure to artificial investment firms totals about $140 billion, but the recent resurgence on Wall Street, driven in large part by a recovery in some of the biggest tech firms, undoubtedly would have lifted that.

In the past week, competition among the tech giants continued, despite the well-aired concerns the whole thing resembles an enormous financial bubble.

Amazon tipped $US5 billion ($7 billion) into AI's rising star, Anthropic. That was on top of the $US8 billion ($11.1 billion) already committed, hinting there was $US20 billion ($28 billion) more to come.

Anthropic, in turn, agreed to spend more than $US100 billion ($140 billion) on Amazon's cloud services over the next decade.

Then, over the weekend, Google stumped up $US10 billion ($14 billion) for Anthropic with another $US30 billion ($42 billion) on offer, strengthening an already close relationship.

Meanwhile, the race to build data centres shows no sign of abating.

Despite the imminent threat of a global energy crisis, there is no shortage of cash being thrown at AI infrastructure firms and the data centres that consume vast quantities of electricity and water.

Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella strutted the stage in Sydney last week, announcing plans to fork out $25 billion over the next three years for a series of data centres on the world's driest continent.

He'll be jostling for space from local contenders like NextDC, Firmus and AirTrunk.

![Long rows of machines stretching into the distance at a Microsoft data center.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6cb0098505f9c997f1fe8a6d03f91510?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1333&cropW=2000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Many companies are building data centres in Australia. (Supplied: Microsoft)

## Xi and Trump have something in common

Tim Cook wasn't the only one in the tech world to find himself at a loose end last week.

Both Facebook parent Meta and Microsoft announced global lay-offs that could cut as many as 23,000 staff, adding to an already extensive list of casualties in the past year.

Has Audio Duration: 8 minutes .

Listen 8m

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/04afde218a223297159fca733275c501?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1688&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=656&width=862&height=485)

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/04afde218a223297159fca733275c501?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1688&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=656&width=862&height=485)

Listen to business team's latest Fuelcast episode, Demand Destruction.

Legitimate fears that AI could kill far more jobs than it creates appear to be reinforced, as the architects behind the revolution have been among the first to be affected.

Even in China, an unexpected surge in unemployment numbers last week, particularly among younger workers, pointed to the sudden arrival of AI.

Youth unemployment in the Middle Kingdom, where official statistics often gloss over economic trends, rose sharply and reached a record 7.7 per cent among those aged 25 to 29.

Citi economists believe this could create concerns within Beijing.

"The unexpected deterioration for early career workers, who are more likely AI exposed, seems to point to the unfolding AI-driven replacement, and we believe the structural headwinds from AI adoption are set to identify," they wrote in a note to clients.

"We remain cautious on the labour market and consumption outlook and see the mid-year Politburo as a plausible venue for policy adjustment."

Meanwhile, in the US, President Donald Trump is facing his own AI problems.

The multi-billion-dollar rush to build data centres may have bolstered US economic growth figures, masking the impact of his tariff policies.

But the shortage of high-technology materials needed for their construction has lit a fire under imports, worsening America's massive trade deficit.

As his struggle to bring the Iran war to an end goes on, gasoline prices continue to escalate. And now his long-promised fix to the US trade deficit is becoming increasingly out of reach.

# Soaring petrol prices force tourism operators to introduce surcharges

By national tourism reporter [Kristy Sexton-McGrath](https://www.abc.net.au/news/kristy-sexton-mcgrath/8272228)

- [Topic:Travel and Tourism](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/travel-and-tourism)

2h ago2 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 2:55pm

![Tourists Byron Thake and Annabelle Martin sit on a boat in Cairns.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/33ad4b33715d7fdae20d14731e28f555?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=675&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=113&width=862&height=485)

Tourists Byron Thake (right) and Annabelle Martin from Wollongong paid a fuel levy for their recent reef trip. (ABC News: Brendan Mounter)

## In short:

Tourism operators on the Great Barrier Reef are calling for COVID-era support amid soaring fuel costs.

Some operators are introducing passenger fuel surcharges, scaling back trips and cutting staff hours, in order to balance the books.

The reef injects about $9 billion a year into the Australian economy, employing 77,000 people, according to federal government figures.

Tourism operators on the Great Barrier Reef are introducing passenger fuel surcharges and cutting services as soaring petrol costs put pressure on Australia's multi-billion-dollar tourism industry.

Tropical island resorts and large passenger boats powered by millions of litres of diesel each year are being pushed to the brink, according to the industry, with calls for COVID-era support for affected workers.

Association of Marine Park Tourism Operators (AMPTO) chief executive Gareth Phillips said operators in Cairns, Port Douglas and the Whitsundays were scaling back trips and cutting staff hours because they could not afford to run their boats.

"We're seeing reductions of 20 to 30 per cent in services," Mr Phillips said.

"Operators still need minimum crew levels for safety, but hours are being cut. That means less income, less certainty, and people starting to leave the industry."

![Gareth Phillips with his arms folded](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d4233f7ed8bc70915b71d44df7abcc17?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

CEO of the Australian Marine Park tourism Organisation Gareth Phillips wants passenger fees waived for the next six months. (ABC News: Brendan Mounter)

Mr Phillips said AMPTO had written to both the federal and Queensland governments, asking them to waive a range of per-passenger charges imposed on operators.

"There's been a lot of discussion, but not a lot of action," he said.

The Great Barrier Reef is a major drawcard for international tourists, injecting about $9 billion a year into the Australian economy, and employing 77,000 people, according to federal government figures.

![A sign informs boat passengers of a fuel surcharge.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e53f963e80703f04dce6067ed48b2b44?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=50&width=862&height=575)

Passengers travelling to the Great Barrier Reef are helping to cover the rising cost by paying surcharges. (ABC News: Brendan Mounter)

Mr Phillips said conditions were tougher than the pandemic, when international border closures brought tourism to a halt.

"During COVID, there was certainty, and there was support," he said.

"Right now, it's the uncertainty around fuel pricing and availability that's alarming."

## Tropical islands under pressure

On Green Island off Cairns, rising costs are filtering through the entire operation.

The Coral Cay is one of the most visited reef destinations in the country and has been welcoming tourists for more than a century.

While it has invested heavily in environmental management, including eco-tourism initiatives and wastewater treatment systems, the operation remains heavily exposed to fuel costs.

![Green island as seen from just above water level.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/45dfceca283d268296991b8508e0f93a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=50&width=862&height=575)

Green Island is one of the most visited reef destinations in the country. (Audience submitted: Seb Ian)

Solar power is used where possible, but dense canopy limits output, meaning most of the island's energy still comes from diesel generators — powering accommodation, kitchens, air-conditioning and essential services.

Quicksilver Group CEO Tony Baker said the company used 300,000 litres of fuel a month across its operations.

"From late February through to the first week of the Easter school holidays, our fuel costs went up 168 per cent," he said.

![Quicksilver CEO Tony Baker stands at a marina near the water](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/893b5a1be4a5167695015d8805d530bc?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=50&width=862&height=575)

Quicksilver CEO Tony Baker says fuel costs are hitting reef operators hard. (ABC News: Brendan Mounter)

Those costs are now being passed on to visitors through fuel surcharges, but Mr Baker said the increase still did not cover total operating costs.

"It's not something we want to do — and it's not something customers want — but sometimes it's necessary," he said.

But he said the bigger issue was unpredictability.

![Piles of dry human waste sit on a bench](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/bb245ae5680aa133b706753ee62359c3?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=50&width=862&height=575)

Green Island Resort off Cairns recycles human waste in a bid to cut down on its carbon footprint. (ABC News: Brendan Mounter)

"Prices are changing week to week, and that makes it very hard when fuel is such a big cost," he said.

"If we don't get minimum numbers to cover the cost of that vessel, we won't operate."

## 'If we don't get the numbers … we won't fly'

Rising fuel costs are also forcing the skydiving industry to rethink how it flies — and how often.

Skydive Australia's Regional Operations Manager for North Queensland, Paul Poulos, said about 20 staff were needed to accommodate between 1,000 and 2,000 jumps a month, depending on weather conditions.

> "We're certainly seeing pressure from rising fuel costs,"

Mr Poulos said.

![Paul Poulos in a casual shirt smiles for the camera](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f62e698e61c68035ec77d66b9c50c3eb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Skydive Australia's regional operations manager for North Queensland, Paul Poulos, says the industry is feeling the pressure. (ABC News: Brendan Mounter)

"That's across aircraft operations, transport and logistics. It flows through everything we do."

While the cost of jumps has held steady for now, he said the focus had shifted to keeping aircraft full and matching schedules to demand.

"We're looking at maximising loads, matching staffing to demand and consolidating operating days where we can," he said.

"If we don't get the numbers to make a flight viable, we won't fly it."

He said it was important to maintain skydiving experiences in regional tourism hubs like Cairns and Airlie Beach.

"These are iconic destinations. People build their holidays around them," he said.

"And if we lose accessibility, it doesn't just impact us, it impacts the whole region."

## Calls for help

Across Australia, regional tourism businesses reported a subdued Easter holiday period, [largely driven by rising fuel prices affecting road trips.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-30/fuel-price-hikes-easter-holiday-travel-iran-war/106494478)

Australian Regional Tourism CEO Lauren Douglass said the industry was "in crisis" and bookings from May onwards were "almost non-existent".

"Demand is weakening in regions more than two hours from capital cities, with bookings down by up to 40 per cent in some areas," Ms Douglass said.

Federal Opposition tourism spokesman David Littleproud said he had written to Tourism Minister Don Farrell calling for a COVID-era response to support regional tourism operators including grants.

"Regional tourism relies on travellers feeling confident to hit the road, to fuel up, and to explore Australia's vast interior. When the government runs campaigns that frame fuel use as something to be avoided or reduced, it sends a message to prospective travellers," Mr Littleproud said.

A federal government spokesman said it was "co-ordinating closely with state and territory governments, and taking every practical step to shield Australians from the worst impacts of the conflict in the Middle East".

# Taroom Trough's oil field touted as solution to fuel security but no one knows how much oil is there

- [![A journalist holding a microphone and smiling. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2d6e3b68fe0df9d324809ea7f1cac590?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=400&cropW=400&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
L\\
\\
By Laura Lavelle](https://www.abc.net.au/news/laura-lavelle/13887120)
- [7.30](https://www.abc.net.au/news/programs/730/)
- [Topic:Oil and Gas](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/oil-and-gas)

14h ago14 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 5:58am

![Men and women wearing hard hats and hi-vis clothing stand in a field.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/49252d70e8f0af7986f5f96578194e6b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=674&cropW=1198&xPos=82&yPos=9&width=862&height=485)

David Crisafulli visits the Taroom Trough with Shell QGC vice-president Krishna Venkatesan. (LinkedIn)

At first glance, it's easy to miss.

A fenced-off patch of land, 130 metres wide and long, carved out of the scrub against a backdrop of bushland in regional Queensland.

At the centre of the site, 75 kilometres from the tiny town of Condamine in the Western Downs, a small wellhead is sunk below surface level.

From above, it appears unassuming.

![A pipe pokes up through a hole in a dusty field.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d61eac5dbc64d852a419e9d17ab15c10?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

A wellhead protrudes from the ground in the Taroom Trough. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

But according to Queensland's premier, sites like this could be the solution to averting a future fuel crisis and a once-in-a-generation opportunity to claw back sovereign capability, because thousands of metres below lies one of the world's most sought-after resources — oil.

This "sea of oil", as Premier David Crisafulli describes it, sits within the Taroom Trough, a petroleum and gas exploration area in south-west Queensland.

Queensland's Department of Resources estimates it spans around 43,000 square kilometres, which is around the size of Denmark.

Visiting one of the exploratory wells earlier this month, the premier labelled the Taroom Trough a "golden opportunity" to extract "liquid gold" at a time when the nation needs fuel security more than ever.

![A semi-trailer driving past a sign that reads "Welcome to Condamine".](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c9fb911803e2d39421aeaaea24ecf686?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The Taroom Trough is 75km from Condamine in regional Queensland. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

The oil itself isn't new. It's been known to exist here for decades. An hour's drive away is the location of Australia's first commercial oil field, at Moonie.

What is new is everything around it — a national spotlight on fuel security and refining capability, and technology that may make the oil worth extracting.

"Sure, it's about fuel. But it's more than that," Mr Crisafulli said.

> "It's about sovereignty, national capability, a time when governments were prepared to control their own destiny."

But experts and some in the industry are urging caution.

There are several hurdles proponents must first overcome before it's "drill, baby, drill", as the current US president likes to say.

## 'We're right on the cusp'

![A man wearing a hi-vis top, smiling.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/540458ce154b12b37c955f26b2d9a617?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Richard Cottee says Elixir Energy is "onto something really good" in the Taroom Trough. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

There are currently four companies holding licences to explore the Taroom Trough.

Elixir Energy is one of the largest landholders. It is run by Queensland gas pioneer Richard Cottee, who was one of the driving forces behind the success of coal seam gas (CSG) in south-west Queensland in the early 2000s, when that project was deemed unproven, niche and risky.

"Everyone said it was never going to work," he told 7.30.

Mr Cottee joined the Queensland Gas Company (QGC) when it was a multi-million-dollar gas player in 2002.

When it sold in the midst of the global financial crisis less than a decade later, it was a $5.7 billion energy giant.

![Pipes stick up out of the ground in a dusty field.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/34f47a158f4c281b09d62ba2995a8f4e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

A wellhead in the ground in the Taroom Trough. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

Mr Cottee says the Taroom Trough is the next frontier. He believes his company, Elixir Energy, can replicate the same success he had in the region with CSG, but with oil.

"The \[indicators\] are even more encouraging than what I saw for coal seam gas in 2002," he said.

> "I honestly believe we are nearly there. We're right on the cusp."

Mr Cottee's company expects it could produce 30 million barrels of oil from its acreage, and enough gas to supply 10 per cent of Australia's eastern seaboard for a decade.

But he does not know exactly how much oil the Taroom Trough may produce.

"All we know is that we are onto something really good. And when you're on a good thing, stick to it," he said.

## Like playing Battleship in the bush

![A man wearing a hard hat and a hi-vis shirt speaks to the media in a disty field.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d827bc943277f77af65a94f34b7117d2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=853&cropW=1280&xPos=0&yPos=42&width=862&height=575)

Krishna Venkatesan says "it's early days" and they don't know how much oil is there. (LinkedIn)

QGC is now operated by Shell, which is also exploring the Taroom Trough.

Shell QGC is currently producing 200 barrels of light oil per day from one of its wells at the site, a drop in the ocean compared with the more than 1 million barrels of oil Australians use every day.

"It's really high quality and that can be used to refine and produce diesel," Shell QGC vice-president Krishna Venkatesan said.

> "More than 50 per cent of what we produce can be refined to diesel and the rest can be used for solvents and other chemical products that can be used for the industry."

Queensland's Resources Department says early technical assessments of the Taroom Trough indicate it could produce up to 1,000 barrels of oil a day per well in the short term.

But the only way to get a better idea of what the potential may be at the peak of production is by drilling more exploratory wells, of which there are already around a dozen.

![A cow in a paddock.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/91b3e1b5c7e03633664f5bfd7843dfc7?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

A cow near Elixir Energy's site at the Taroom Trough. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

It's a bit like playing Battleship in the bush, hitting different sites to see what's there.

Elixir Energy and Shell QGC say that in all wells they've drilled so far, oil has been struck, but in an interview with 7.30, Mr Venkatesan wasn't as optimistic about the Taroom Trough project as the premier.

"We've been quite clear at this point in time, it's early days," Mr Venkatesan said.

"We don't know how much we have in ground today and hopefully in the next six to 12 months we will be able to get a view.

"It's now about the scale and for that scale to happen you need a significant level of investment, and that investment can only come through once you understand what's … the total the basin can offer.

"If we can unlock this basin, and I believe the reserves are there … this could be part of the solution to meet the Australian energy needs."

## 'More work required' to confirm amount of oil

![An oil pump.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2456f509bd3c3e97a803f66d71978ff2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

An original oil pump from the Moonie oil fields of the 1960s. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

One thing proponents and experts agree on is that accessing the oil won't be easy or cheap.

The resource is trapped more than 3,000 metres below the surface, much deeper than the CSG wells currently scattered across the region, which are typically around a few hundred metres deep.

Once at the depth of the oil, horizontal drilling begins, continuing for a couple of kilometres before the rock is hydraulically fractured (fracked).

Navigating the depths of the Taroom Trough has been likened to keyhole surgery on a much larger scale.

> "In Queensland we haven't drilled long horizontal sections, and this will absolutely open up the basin differently,"

Mr Venkatesan said.

Despite the challenges that lay ahead, Mr Cottee rejects the notion that the Taroom Trough project may be a gamble.

![A danger sign warns of a gas pipeline in the ground.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/80b82f0992ba4ece6352b9aac8e898b6?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Gas is also found on the Taroom Trough. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

"I don't think it's a gamble at all. I really think Shell has totally de-risked it. We are going to be de-risking it," he said.

> "If you are willing to dream, and there's a lot of work yet to go, this province could … change the total dynamics, but only if we can get government approvals."

University of Queensland adjunct professor Rick Wilkinson, who also runs an independent energy advisory firm, says initial drilling has been positive but the project isn't without risks.

"As yet there are no certified economic oil and gas resources in the Taroom Trough," Mr Wilkinson said.

"More work is still required to prove the size of these resources, and that they can be produced economically."

![A portrait photo of a man with neatly trimmed white hair and glasses, in a suit and tie.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/de2ccdfb7802cb405175861bb21b1846?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1066&cropW=1599&xPos=0&yPos=214&width=862&height=575)

Rick Wilkinson says more work is needed to "prove the size of these resources". (Supplied: Rick Wilkinson)

He said accessing the oil could be up to 10 times more expensive than drilling a coal seam gas well.

"The technology \[needs to be more advanced\] and the costs are much higher when you work at that depth," he said.

Mr Wilkinson said considering the challenges and potential output in mind, he did not think the Taroom Trough was the "solution" to the fuel crisis, but rather an important contribution.

> "In Australia we use about 1 million barrels of oil per day … about 90 per cent of that comes from overseas,"

he said.

"Anything that we can add \[to stock the remaining two\] refineries has got to be a positive. But 350-odd-million barrels of oil per year — that's really a stretch target for the Taroom Trough."

## Push to fast-track approvals

As the hype around the Taroom Trough builds, the debate has spilled beyond the basin, triggering a political stand-off between Queensland's premier and the federal environment minister.

Mr Crisafulli wants the federal government to streamline environmental approvals for the project, arguing it's in the national interest.

![A man in suit, tie and glasses standing in front of a furled flag.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/bfc071d37ee46095c871cf307a2b6d18?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2872&cropW=4308&xPos=397&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Murray Watt has accused the Queensland premier of being "all talk and no action". (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

Last week the premier said Queensland had removed barriers that would prevent resources from getting to market, designating the Taroom Trough a "coordinated project".

But Mr Crisafulli said there was "one missing piece of the puzzle" — he wants the federal government to remove a condition from new environmental laws, passed in November, which exempt fossil fuel projects from being fast-tracked under the national interest framework.

But federal Environment Minister Murray Watt said he had not received a proposal from the premier, accusing him of being "all talk and no action".

"The premier in Queensland has been very keen to get out and do photo opportunities and media statements about a possible oil development," Mr Watt said.

> "He's yet to provide a single piece of information to us that we can consider, but he wants us to fast-track a development that he hasn't got any information about."

Mr Watt wrote to Mr Crisafulli last week asking for clarity. Mr Watt told 7.30 he had yet to receive a response, but he had received a letter from Queensland's Deputy Premier Jarrod Bleijie, urging him to change environmental laws.

There's a lot riding on what happens next.

For proponents, millions of dollars in exploration costs have already been spent and they hope the project is viable enough to reclaim some of those funds.

For the state, billions of dollars in royalties stand to be made.

And for communities across the Western Downs, the possibility of jobs and a new wave of investment.

![A man wearing a collared shirt and suit jacket, smiling.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/45b26b181e6b3fdd3ff58d371c8255c7?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Andrew Smith is hopeful the Taroom Trough will bring more jobs to the region. (ABC News: Chris Gillette)

Western Downs Mayor Andrew Smith said the gas industry had transformed the region and the production of oil would keep the momentum going.

"If we go back 30 years ago, things were pretty tough. There was no diversity in our economies. We were looking at the skies nearly every day," he said.

"I can remember a time when parents were sort of saying to their children, 'Don't come home. There's not much to come home to.'

"\[With oil\] we know that there'll be jobs, we know that there'll be business opportunities, but we are seeing it as potentially our next wave of investment."

**Watch** [**7.30**](https://iview.abc.net.au/show/7-30) **, Mondays to Thursdays 7:30pm on** [**ABC iview**](https://iview.abc.net.au/show/7-30) **and ABC TV**

# Resources industry in the spotlight during Angus Taylor's first trip to WA as Opposition leader

By WA state political reporter [Keane Bourke](https://www.abc.net.au/news/keane-bourke/12799768)

- [Topic:Government and Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/government-and-politics)

1h ago1 hours agoSat 25 Apr 2026 at 8:41pm

![Angus Taylor speaking to media standing outside a building.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a326f05e38d77132eeb54e24e7c2f3e2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2667&cropW=4000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Angus Taylor visits WA for the first time as Liberal leader. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

The true power of one of the biggest political forces in Western Australia was on full display this week.

Not any individual person, or even company, but rather the irresistible pull of the state's massive, profitable resources industry.

On at least one issue, it even seemed to pull Labor Premier Roger Cook's views closer to those of Liberal Opposition Leader Angus Taylor than his own colleagues.

And that wasn't even the only sign of tension between state and federal Labor this week

![Federal Opposition Leader Angus Taylor gives a speech while standing in front of a crowd of people.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/37b3f036f65595be74604116150d5725?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2197&cropW=3296&xPos=277&yPos=176&width=862&height=575)

Angus Taylor gives a speech at the Chamber of Minerals and Energy of WA event in Perth. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

After jetting into Perth for the first time since becoming opposition leader, Taylor made a beeline for the state's resources movers and shakers.

He ticked all the boxes to be accepted in the state: backing in the state's GST deal and thanking the mining industry because it "elevates everyone else's prosperity".

As you'd expect, he was quick to reject any idea of a 25 per cent tax on gas exports — an idea explored by a [Greens-led parliamentary inquiry this week.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-24/woodside-submissions-senate-inquiry-gas-tax/106602472)

It's an identical position to Cook's.

"I understand that that sort of conversation has superficial appeal," the premier said on Tuesday.

"But can I just make the observation, is that we are asking companies to come to Australia and invest significant amounts of money as part of a multi-decade project which will see prosperity brought to this country."

## Environmental finger-pointing

Where the pair differed was on how to handle changes to national [environmental laws passed by Labor with the support of the Greens last year.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-11-27/labor-strikes-greens-deal-to-end-environmental-stalemate/106054928)

Neither Cook, nor Taylor, is happy with the laws of which the premier [helped kill off an earlier version.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-11-27/albanese-kills-environmental-protection-reforms/104651976)

The Liberal leader's pledge this week was to increase investment by encouraging more exploration, introducing national priority projects to fast-track environmental approvals and reversing those changes — which he said Labor was entirely responsible for.

![Angus Taylor standing and speaking in a radio studio.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/8b3a40e5b793c6a1ce9dab4237f7775f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2667&cropW=4000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Fast-tracked environmental approvals and encouraging more exploration are on Angus Taylor's agenda. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

"Our job is to reject bad legislation and this was bad legislation on many counts because it's making it harder to invest in our country," he told 102.5 ABC Perth.

But Cook was pointing the finger at Taylor, saying "his team sold Western Australia down the river when they refused to negotiate on sensible reforms" and allowed the Greens to get the government's ear.

![Murray Watt and Roger Cook stand at podiums](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e27fcf04ba50c0946cafc8315481ef44?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2667&cropW=4000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Murray Watt (left) and Premier Cook (right) fronted the media to tout their deal. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

It made for a slightly awkward press conference on Tuesday, when Cook stood shoulder-to-shoulder with Environment Minister Murray Watt — the man who got the changes through parliament after more than five years of attempts by both sides of politics.

The premier awkwardly danced around how much he didn't like the laws, instead trying to focus on a deal signed with Watt for the state and Commonwealth to negotiate how to streamline environmental assessments by the end of the year.

## Butting heads over aged care

WA Health Minister Meredith Hammat also wasn't full of love for her federal colleagues.

States and territories have butted heads over aged care for a long time, with WA's leaders among those saying the Commonwealth's under-funding of the sector is leaving hundreds of older people languishing in hospital beds with nowhere else to go.

"Older people have a right to access health care in hospitals and hospitals have always had older people in them, so I don't always appreciate the language that's being used" was how Federal Aged Care minister Sam Rae responded when asked about the challenge this week.

![A close-up for a woman wearing glasses](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ec25352765484ec34d0ff991bda9fd29?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1280&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

There was tension between Meredith Hammat's approach to aged care, comapred to her federal counterpart. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

Hammat was quick to snap back that "it's been explained to him a number of times".

"Those comments are not helpful in terms of being clear about what is the issue that we're trying to find a solution for," she said.

Asked if it suggests Rae doesn't understand the issue, Hammat said: "I'm happy to explain it to him again."

Perhaps not helping things were the 10 minutes she had spent, largely unsuccessfully, trying to explain why the government could not say how much 60 beds at Midland Hospital will cost to operate when they are expected to come into public hands in August.

![An older person's hands sitting on their lap as they sit in a motorised wheelchair.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/367730757ab0dd965d8a54a4f1af9afa?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2560&cropW=3840&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

WA and the Commonwealth aren't in agreement about the best aged care approach. (ABC News: Lily Nothling)

Long story short, there is an estimate based on a "complex formula" and "weighted activity units" and plenty of other factors.

But because beds can be used for so many things and each attracts a different level of funding, it's too difficult to provide an estimate ahead of time.

It does make it a bit hard to talk about a budget announcement when there's no specific money in the budget for it though.

## Protest 'ratbag'

New Woodside CEO Liz Westcott would have found it a bit difficult to talk this week too, with her address to the company's annual general meeting interrupted by dozens of protesters playing whale songs, singing, blowing whistles and even storming the stage.

![A woman with a white blazer sits at bench on a stage. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/27d32abcc82ecf25fed9ff8a8e702f54?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Liz Westcott fronts the Woodside AGM. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

Around 30 were given move-on notices by police for their shenanigans.

Activists were this year voicing their opposition to the company developing gas fields in the Browse Basin off the Kimberley coast.

It is one of the country's biggest untapped resource projects, and will be a crucial supply for the company's North West Shelf gas plant, which the federal government last year [approved to operate until 2070.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-09-12/woodside-north-west-shelf-decision-murray-watt-pilbara/105758850)

Opposition to the project is based not only on the emissions associated with producing and using the gas, but also because of the [basin's proximity to the Scott Reef](https://6.71.142.38/), which is home to endangered whales, turtles and thousands of corals.

![Protesters hold placards such as 'Hands Off Scott Reef'](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/62770bb952441e9fee2c417410edc998?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The Woodside AGM is met with oppoisition from protesters. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

Westcott insists any operations will not impact the reef, a claim disputed by organisations like Greenpeace.

Among those so committed to opposing the project they were slapped with a move-on notice was Greens MLC Sophie McNeil.

Asked about it, the premier reflected on a "ratbag" who would "protest at the drop of a hat" in his university days by the name of Roger Cook.

He said he supported people's right to protest as long as it respected other people's rights, but hinted at his support for Browse and other gas projects which "are important for the state's prosperity".

![A protester hold up a sign stating 'Hands off Scott Reef' while standing in front of a giant inflatable whale](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/7a07be037107fe062b7af100640287bb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

While Premier Cook supports the right to protest, he considers gas projects important to WA. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

"They're also important for our personal, well, our domestic gas supply," he said.

An innocent slip of the tongue, almost certainly, but one which could easily be seen as Freudian by those so inclined.

Especially in a state like Western Australia, where one industry rules above all others.
